text: Hi, this is Abu Bakr Ali. I'm presenting a case of a truncal vagotomy in Heineken-Mickles pyloriplasty with a misdiagnosis and a complication. This is a patient with a complex surgical history. He was involved in a car accident. He had a Missed a bowel injury that led to him undergoing an X-lap. Abdomen was open for two weeks. It was closed again. He leaked again. Abdomen again open for two weeks. From the car accident, he ended up paraplegic, requiring fixation of his vertebrae. And one of the screws kind of got misplaced and had to be revised. He also had some sternal fixation, had an aortic transection requiring an aortic graft.

So, um, uh, about seven years later, he presented to the hospital with the, uh, uh, uh, with the perforated ulcer, uh, because the fear of him having all of these surgery, uh, he was treated kind of conservatively. He ended up with, uh, gastric outlet obstruction requiring a PEG tube place and I got involved for an esophageal bad esophageal stricture initial was thought to be cancer turned out to be a peptic stricture and requiring multiple dilation So I kind of felt the only way of getting him through is fixing his hiatal hernia and dealing with the gastric outlet obstruction. By performing Heineken-Mickle's pyloriplasty felt that the vagotomy will reduce the acid, fixing the hiatal hernia will also reduce the acid reflux.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic dissection of the hiatal hernia, identifying and dissecting the anterior and posterior vagus nerves. A pin rose is used to retract and apply tension to locate the posterior vagus, which is confirmed through frozen section analysis.

The surgeon identifies the posterior vagus nerve, which is located to the right of the esophagus, amidst a significant amount of inflammatory tissue. Despite aggressive PPI management, the tissue appears inflamed. The dissection is challenging, particularly on the left side of the esophagus where a mucosal tear was previously noted. The posterior dissection is less problematic, although potential adhesions are anticipated due to the presence of an aortic stent.

The surgeon navigates behind the left atrium, encountering a vessel with clear fluid, possibly a lymphatic duct. An EGD is performed, and the robotic camera is switched to near-infrared mode to assess the area. The stomach and esophagus are visible, showing significant inflammation but no evidence of leakage.

The surgeon closes the hiatus using Ethabon sutures with Pledgets, ensuring adequate intraabdominal esophageal length after anterior vagotomy. The duodenum is retracted to the patient's right, revealing the colon beneath. Co-ochorization is performed until the left IVC is visible joining the inferior vena cava. The surgeon prepares for Heineken-Michels pyloriplasty, using ICG mode to locate the common bile duct and cold scissors to separate adhered tissues.

The surgeon begins by opening the pylorus from the stomach side towards the duodenum, encountering a pinhole stricture. A mass is identified and excised from the area of the stricture, which is then sent to pathology. The mass is approximately 2 by 3 centimeters and is later identified as pancreatic tissue. The mucosa is re-approximated using vial sutures. The surgeon ensures the common bile duct is not injured and uses an EGD scope to confirm the patency of the connection between the stomach and duodenum.

At this point, I proceeded with the Heineken-Michels pyeloplasty in the usual fashion. So this, I mean, the surgery itself was about three and a half hours. Blood loss, despite what it looks, it wasn't that much, to be honest with you. He was doing very well post-operatively until post-op day number two, where he started getting like short of breath. We got an x-ray. There was a lot of fluid. I immediately placed a chest tube and there was like four liters of fluid coming out from the left side and about a liter coming out of the right side. So I was thinking immediately, did I injure the thoracic duct? So I sent for a triglyceride. It's a send-out test. It takes about five days. I sent the fluid for amylase. The initial fluid amylase was normal. I got a swallow study. The initial swallow study was normal.

and so at this point I proceeded with IR embolization of the thoracic duct they did the study and there was no evidence of leak repeat amylase actually was extremely elevated so it turned out that he had an esophageal injury right where I initially showed you where I was dissecting and it was about a one point five centimeter injury and this was not discovered like it was discovered pretty late I mean He was not on any pressers or anything. I mean, this guy was talking. He was just having like about three liters coming out of his chest tube. It was not bilious. So I proceeded with stenting him. He has a drain. He's on a regular diet, and he's doing pretty much well, and I'll be exchanging the stent very soon. Thank you.
